one_line_takeaway: By surgically editing a public dense retriever with contrastive model editing so it surfaces an SCA-suppressing instruction only for a chosen victim query, DISARMRAG defeats LLM self-correction and pushes RAG attack success rates above 90% while remaining nearly undetectable on benign retrieval, fluency, and parameter-spectrum checks.
key_finding: DISARMRAG near-perfectly retrieves the malicious instruction (TI Recall@k >96% across datasets) and achieves ASR exceeding 90% under diverse SCA-activating prompts, far above baselines that collapse under SCA. On NQ/GPT-4o mini it reaches 94.00% ASR vs 48.33% for the strongest baseline PoisonedRAG(W). It also defeats reasoning models (94.75% on DeepSeek R1, 93.00% on QwQ, where baselines reach only ~20-31%) and generalizes across five optimized defensive prompts (90-93% on GPT-4o mini, 82-91% on Deepseek V3). Stealth is strong: benign BEIR retrieval degrades <1% (e.g., NQ Recall@100 -1.2%, NDCG@100 -1.0%), the injected instruction has benign-like perplexity (36 vs GASLITE's 2818) and lexical density (0.43), it survives paraphrasing defenses (ASR >81%, TI Recall@k >99%), and the edited retriever's singular-value spectrum is statistically indistinguishable from benign (sharpness/cumE identical, KL/JS ~0). Ablations show every AC component and the query-shift regularizer matter (removing the regularizer drops benign NQ recall by 67%; removing AC3 drops GPT-4o mini ASR from 94% to 46%).
core_contribution: The paper introduces DISARMRAG, a new RAG-poisoning paradigm that compromises the RETRIEVER itself (not just the knowledge base) in order to suppress the self-correction ability (SCA) of modern LLMs and force attacker-chosen answers. Two of the paper's own contributions underpin this: (1) a preliminary empirical study showing that prior knowledge-base poisoning attacks (PoisonedRAG, GASLITE, prompt injection, disinformation) are largely neutralized once the LLM is given SCA-activating system prompts, with average ASR dropping from 82.3% to 22.3%; and (2) the DISARMRAG attack, which combines a contrastive-learning-based model-editing (ME) technique that surgically edits a public retriever so it returns an attacker-specified malicious instruction ONLY for a chosen victim query (while leaving benign retrieval intact), plus an iterative attacker/defender prompt co-optimization framework that discovers instructions robust across LLMs and defensive prompts. Note the hypernetwork-based model-editing machinery itself is adapted from prior work (Meng et al. ROME/MEMIT, and hypernet editors refs [52],[70]); the paper's novelty is redirecting it to poison bidirectional retriever encoders via a contrastive objective plus a query-embedding-stability regularizer.
threat_model: Attacker's goal: make a security-aware RAG system (one whose system prompt explicitly activates SCA, telling the LLM to verify/reject false context) output an attacker-chosen answer for a specific victim query. Capabilities: WHITE-BOX access to the retriever only. The attacker downloads a publicly available retriever (e.g., NVIDIA ChatRTX-style deployments using open-source Contriever), edits/fine-tunes it to embed the malicious behavior, and redistributes it as a drop-in replacement on platforms like Hugging Face; this is framed as a 'repackaging attack' from the software-security literature. The attacker also injects adversarial contexts into the knowledge base. Crucially, the attacker has NO access to the knowledge-base contents (cannot inspect/modify), CANNOT query the LLM, and CANNOT see its system prompt. The attack is targeted: it requires knowing the victim query in advance.
limitations: 1) The threat model requires WHITE-BOX retriever access plus the ability to get a poisoned retriever adopted by the victim (supply-chain/repackaging assumption) - stronger than pure data-poisoning attacks. 2) The attack is TARGETED and per-query: it needs the victim query known in advance and a single-shot edit per query; it is not a broad, query-agnostic compromise. 3) Malicious-context retrieval is imperfect - MC F1 averages ~0.77 (0.8 default), so not every retrieved context is adversarial, and ASR degrades as k grows (though still ~2-2.5x baselines). 4) Evaluated only on Contriever-family dense retrievers and closed-ended QA; generalization to other retriever architectures or open-ended tasks is untested. 5) Defenses are evaluated by the authors themselves; cross-encoder re-ranking and pattern filters are argued away qualitatively (cost/keyword-avoidance) rather than empirically defeated. 6) The paper proposes the attack but offers no concrete retriever-centric defense, only a call for one.

datasets_used: BEIR benchmark (FEVER, FiQA, HotpotQA, MSMARCO, NQ, SciFact, Webis) - for benign-retrieval stealth evaluation
quantitative_results: Preliminary study: SCA-activating prompts drop average ASR of prior attacks from 82.3% to 22.3%
DISARMRAG TI Recall@k >96% across datasets; TI Recall@1 near 100%
DeepSeek R1 NQ ASR 94.75%, QwQ 93.00% (baselines ~20.5% and ~30.5%)
HotpotQA/GPT-4o mini 84.33%, MS MARCO/GPT-4o mini 86.75% (best baseline 59.00% and 42.67%)
Average malicious-context F1 (MC F1) ~77.33% (default 0.8 at k=5)
Across 5 optimized defensive prompts DP1-DP5: GPT-4o mini 90-93%, Deepseek V3 min 82%, GPT-OSS ~90-93%
BEIR benign retrieval degradation <1% (NQ NDCG@100 -1.0%, Recall@100 -1.2%; other datasets avg ~0.22%)

Textual metrics: DISARMRAG instruction perplexity 36 / lexical density 0.43 vs GASLITE 2818 / 0.62 and Hotflip 163; DISARMRAG keeps ASR 94% under perplexity and lexical-density checks while GASLITE ASR falls to 35%
Paraphrasing defense: ASR still >81%, TI Recall@k >99% (NQ ASR 82.8 with defense vs 94.0 without)
Ablation: removing query-shift regularizer (wo. Const.) drops NQ benign recall -67.1%; Cosine(D) direct-training drops FEVER recall -77.1%
AC ablation (GPT-4o mini): full 94%, removing AC2 -> 67%, removing AC3 -> 46%
Spectral detection: sharpness 0.01, cumE(r32) 0.20 identical for benign vs edited retriever
Editor trained on only 100 samples; single-shot edit per victim query; top-k=5, 100 victim queries per dataset
baselines_compared: Poisoning-design variants Cosine(D), Cosine(E), and 'wo. Const.' (stealth ablation)
